message.content: As of 2026, have any organizations created inventories of tasks done by AI or assisted by AI that measure how advanced they are in cognitive capability or how deep a level of reasoning they require?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"As AI becomes cognitive infrastructure, policy-makers must govern for resilience | World Economic Forum","url":"https://www.weforum.org/stories/2026/03/ai-cognitive-infrastructure-policy-resilience/"},{"title":"Measuring Progress Towards AGI: A Cognitive Framework","url":"https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/google-deepmind/measuring-agi-cognitive-framework/"},{"title":"AI in 2026: From Breakthrough Illusions to Operational Reality - hyperight.com","url":"https://hyperight.com/2026-ai-depth-over-hype/"},{"title":"Ultimate Guide - The Best LLMs for Reasoning Tasks in 2026","url":"https://www.siliconflow.com/articles/en/best-LLMs-for-reasoning-tasks"},{"title":"Top 10 Best AI Reasoning Models in 2026 - TechNow","url":"https://tech-now.io/en/blogs/top-10-best-ai-reasoning-models-in-2026"},{"title":"AI Reasoning Models 2026: From OpenAI o3 to DeepSeek-R1 and the Test-Time Compute Revolution | Zylos Research","url":"https://zylos.ai/research/2026-01-24-ai-reasoning-models"},{"title":"The Cognitive Capabilities of Generative AI: A Comparative ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.07391"},{"title":"General scales unlock AI evaluation with explanatory and predictive power | Nature","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10303-2"},{"title":"LLM Benchmarks 2026 - Compare AI Benchmarks and Tests","url":"https://llm-stats.com/benchmarks"},{"title":"AI Reasoning 2026, Theory of Mind, Chain of Thought, and the Path to Genuine Understanding - Pooya Golchian","url":"https://pooya.blog/blog/ai-reasoning-systems-theory-of-mind-2026/"}]

Based on the web search results, here's an inventory of tasks done by AI cognitive capability with reasoning depth in 2026:

## Key Cognitive Capabilities and Tasks in 2026

### Primary Reasoning Tasks

AI systems are being evaluated across cognitive abilities including reasoning (drawing valid conclusions through logical inference), metacognition (knowledge and monitoring of one's own cognitive processes), executive functions (planning, inhibition and cognitive flexibility), problem solving (finding effective solutions to domain-specific problems), and social cognition (processing and interpreting social information).

### Advanced Reasoning Applications

Advanced reasoning models can understand context, make logical inferences, solve mathematical and scientific problems, and even plan multi-stage tasks autonomously. More specifically, many enterprise tasks require endurance, planning, revising assumptions, and navigating ambiguity over time.

### Improved Cognitive Infrastructure

AI systems shape how people search for information, draft arguments, plan projects, evaluate risks and make decisions. Google Gemini 2.5/3 introduces dynamic "thinking mode" that automatically adjusts reasoning effort based on task complexity, with separate Flash (fast reasoning) and Pro (deep reasoning) variants.

### Specific Problem-Solving Capabilities

Self-reflection (recognizing and correcting mistakes mid-reasoning), strategy adaptation (trying different approaches when stuck), multi-step planning (breaking complex problems into manageable subtasks), and verification loops (checking answers before committing) represent the types of reasoning tasks AI systems now perform.

### Reasoning Depth Challenges

However, there is growing recognition that today's reasoning is shallow and often resembles pattern completion more than deliberation, with models struggling with sustained, multi-hour problem-solving without human intervention.

I've performed the web search for your query about organization AI task taxonomy cognitive complexity classification for 2025-2026. Here are the key findings:

## Recent Research on AI Task Taxonomies

A unified taxonomy for agentic AI systems breaks agents into six main dimensions: Perception, Brain, Planning, Action, Tool Use, and Collaboration, along with Core Components, Cognitive Architecture, Learning, Multi Agent Systems, Environments, and Evaluation.

## Human-AI Task Organization

Recent research explores how complex strategies are broken down into discrete tasks, including the implications of AI capable of "super automation"—when it can devise the problem, break the problem into constituent component tasks, and perform those tasks. In human-AI interaction, a human-AI task is defined as a set of human-AI activities undertaken to achieve a specified goal.

## Cognitive Complexity in AI Collaboration

Research shows that both increased task complexity and AI advice faultiness independently impair human-AI-team performance, contributing to a refined understanding of cognitive dynamics in human-AI teaming and offering practical insights for designing AI systems that support effective decision-making under pressure.

## Taxonomy of AI Creativity and Intelligence

Recent frameworks develop systematic taxonomies of creativity—from recombination to transformation—highlighting the mechanisms required for human-like creative abilities. Additionally, in the context of generative AI, creation has been reconceived from the pinnacle of cognitive complexity to a component of vertical helix spirals representing continuous cycles of judgment, revision, and synthesis as learners develop expertise in both content and human-AI collaboration.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "METR task suite AI agent time horizon reasoning complexity 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Task-Completion Time Horizons of Frontier AI Models - METR","url":"https://metr.org/time-horizons/"},{"title":"METR","url":"https://metr.org/"},{"title":"A new Moore's Law for AI agents - AI Digest","url":"https://theaidigest.org/time-horizons"},{"title":"METR Time Horizons | Epoch AI","url":"https://epoch.ai/benchmarks/metr-time-horizons"},{"title":"Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks - METR","url":"https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/"},{"title":"Time Horizon 1.1 - METR","url":"https://metr.org/blog/2026-1-29-time-horizon-1-1/"},{"title":"Clarifying limitations of time horizon","url":"https://metr.org/notes/2026-01-22-time-horizon-limitations/"},{"title":"This is the most misunderstood graph in AI | MIT Technology Review","url":"https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/02/05/1132254/this-is-the-most-misunderstood-graph-in-ai/"},{"title":"Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2503.14499v1"},{"title":"GPT-5.2 Autonomy: Leading The METR’s Time Horizon Test | by Jatin Prasad | Coding Nexus | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/coding-nexus/gpt-5-2-autonomy-leading-the-metrs-time-horizon-test-83d132b92c10"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about METR's task suite for AI agent time horizon reasoning and complexity in 2026:

## Overview of METR's Task Suite

The task-completion time horizon is the task duration (measured by human expert completion time) at which an AI agent is predicted to succeed with a given level of reliability. For example, the 50%-time horizon is the duration at which an agent is predicted to succeed half the time.

## Task Suite Composition

The evaluation uses performance on over a hundred diverse software tasks, and tasks are drawn from RE-Bench, HCAST, and a set of shorter novel software tasks. More recently, METR increased the tasks in their suite by 34% (228/170) and doubled the number of tasks that are 8 hours or longer (31/14).

## Methodology

To estimate the time horizons of frontier AI agents, METR first estimates the duration it takes a human expert to complete each task. For each agent, they fit a logistic curve to predict the probability it successfully completes tasks as a function of human task duration. To get the 50%-time horizon, they find the task duration where the fitted curve intersects with 50% success probability.

## Reasoning and Complexity Improvements

Progress appears to be driven by several key factors: improved logical reasoning capabilities, better tool use capabilities, and greater reliability and self-awareness in task execution.

I found comprehensive results about the OECD AI Capability Indicators and their connection to occupational tasks. Here's what the search revealed:

## OECD AI Capability Indicators Overview

The OECD's beta AI Capability Indicators are designed to assess and compare AI advancements against human abilities. Developed over five years by a collaboration of over 50 experts, the indicators cover nine human abilities, from Language to Manipulation.

## Nine Capability Domains

The nine indicators cover a range of human abilities that each describes the development of AI towards full human equivalence: Language; Social interaction; Problem solving; Creativity; Metacognition and critical thinking; Knowledge, learning and memory; Vision; Manipulation; and Robotic intelligence. The indicators are presented in scales of five levels, where the most challenging capabilities for AI systems are found towards the top.

## Connection to Occupational Tasks

Mapping indicators to occupational and task requirements and the resulting "gap" analysis can be the starting point to analyse how particular occupations may evolve as AI becomes able to help or replace workers for some tasks. The O*NET system includes data for about 900 occupations and its taxonomy has been adopted in part or compared to the occupational taxonomies used by many other countries. O*NET and the other occupational databases directly modelled on it comprise a set of occupational characteristics – including detailed tasks, work environment and required human abilities – that can be directly compared to the AI Capability Indicators.

The framework was released in June 2025 and provides policymakers with an evidence-based tool for understanding AI's potential impact on work and education.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about AI task inventories and measuring workplace cognitive demands:

## Framework for Measuring AI's Occupational Impact

A comprehensive framework analyzes the impact of AI on occupations by mapping 59 generic tasks from worker surveys and occupational databases to 14 cognitive abilities extracted from cognitive science literature, and these to 328 AI benchmarks used to evaluate research intensity across different AI areas.

## Cognitive Abilities Framework

The cognitive abilities are grouped into three categories: (1) people, (2) ideas, and (3) things. The dominant abilities include visual processing, attention and search, comprehension and compositional expression, conceptualization, learning and abstraction, and quantitative and logical reasoning.

## Reasoning Depth Measurement

More recent approaches offer advanced measurement methodologies. General scales for AI evaluation have been introduced that elicit demand profiles explaining what capabilities benchmarks measure, extract ability profiles quantifying AI systems' general strengths and limits, and build on 18 rubrics capturing a broad range of cognitive and intellectual demands. These scales use a quantitative range between 0 and 5+, with 0 representing absence of demand and values 1-4 representing increasing demand levels of capability.

## Intermediate Layer Advantage

Using cognitive abilities as an intermediate layer instead of mapping work tasks to AI benchmarks directly allows for identification of potential AI exposure for tasks for which AI applications have not been explicitly created.

The cognitive abilities are grouped into three categories: (1) people, (2) ideas, and (3) things. The dominant abilities include visual processing, attention and search, comprehension and compositional expression, conceptualization, learning and abstraction, and quantitative and logical reasoning.

## Reasoning Depth Measurement

More recent approaches offer advanced measurement methodologies. General scales for AI evaluation have been introduced that elicit demand profiles explaining what capabilities benchmarks measure, extract ability profiles quantifying AI systems' general strengths and limits, and build on 18 rubrics capturing a broad range of cognitive and intellectual demands. These scales use a quantitative range between 0 and 5+, with 0 representing absence of demand and values 1-4 representing increasing demand levels of capability.

## Intermediate Layer Advantage

Using cognitive abilities as an intermediate layer instead of mapping work tasks to AI benchmarks directly allows for identification of potential AI exposure for tasks for which AI applications have not been explicitly created.

content.text:
Yes. Several organizations have built such inventories by 2026. Notable ones:

- **OECD AI Capability Indicators** (June 2025) — 9 human ability domains (Language, Problem solving, Metacognition & critical thinking, Creativity, Social interaction, Knowledge/learning/memory, Vision, Manipulation, Robotic intelligence), each on a 5-level scale of increasing difficulty, mapped to ~900 O*NET occupations and their tasks.
- **EU JRC framework** (Tolan et al.) — maps 59 generic worker tasks → 14 cognitive abilities (visual processing, comprehension, conceptualization, quantitative/logical reasoning, etc.) → 328 AI benchmarks, letting researchers grade reasoning depth required.
- **METR Time Horizons** (2025–2026, updated v1.1) — inventories 228 tasks (RE-Bench, HCAST, novel software tasks) graded by human-expert completion time, used as a proxy for cognitive complexity / sustained reasoning.
- **"General scales" (Zhou et al., Nature 2026)** — 18 cognitive-demand rubrics scoring tasks/benchmarks 0–5+ (attention, abstraction, planning depth, etc.) to extract demand and ability profiles.
- **Google DeepMind "Measuring Progress Towards AGI"** — cognitive framework spanning reasoning, metacognition, executive function, problem solving, social cognition.
- **NIST AI 200-1 AI Use Taxonomy** and the **Human–AI Task Tensor** (arXiv 2503.15490) — taxonomic, more descriptive than capability-graded.

So the answer is yes — the OECD, EU JRC, METR, and academic groups have all published task inventories explicitly graded by cognitive level or reasoning depth.

message.content: Do any of the frameworks involve a hierarchy of reasoning or hierarchy of capabilities?
<system-reminder>Respond with just the action or changes and without a thinking block, unless this is a redesign or requires fresh reasoning.</system-reminder>

content.text:
Yes — most are explicitly hierarchical:

- **OECD AI Capability Indicators** — each of the 9 ability domains uses a **5-level scale** from rudimentary to full human equivalence, with higher levels requiring deeper reasoning (e.g. Problem solving and Metacognition & critical thinking ladder up from pattern matching to autonomous strategy revision).
- **General Scales (Zhou et al., Nature 2026)** — 18 cognitive-demand rubrics each scored on a **0 to 5+ ordinal hierarchy**, where 0 = no demand and successive levels denote increasing depth of the underlying capability.
- **METR Time Horizons** — implicit hierarchy by **human-expert task duration** (seconds → minutes → hours → 8+ hours), used as a proxy for sustained reasoning depth; reported as 50%/80% reliability horizons.
- **Google DeepMind "Levels of AGI"** — 6-level hierarchy (No AI → Emerging → Competent → Expert → Virtuoso → Superhuman) crossed with a Narrow/General axis.
- **EU JRC framework** — layered rather than ranked: tasks → 14 cognitive abilities → benchmarks, but the cognitive abilities themselves draw on Cattell–Horn–Carroll (CHC) theory, which is hierarchical (general → broad → narrow abilities).
- **Bloom's Taxonomy revisions for AI** — recent edu-policy work (e.g. EdWeek 2026) re-stacks Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create against AI capabilities.
- **NIST AI 200-1** — taxonomic/categorical, **not** a capability hierarchy.

So the OECD, DeepMind, and General Scales frameworks are the most explicitly hierarchical for reasoning/capability depth.

Sources:
- [OECD AI Capability Indicators – full report](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/2025/06/introducing-the-oecd-ai-capability-indicators_7c0731f0/full-report/component-5.html)
- [General scales – Nature 2026](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10303-2)
- [Measuring Progress Towards AGI – DeepMind](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/google-deepmind/measuring-agi-cognitive-framework/)
- [METR Time Horizons](https://metr.org/time-horizons/)
- [JRC: Measuring the Occupational Impact of AI](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1613/jair.1.12647)
- [Bloom's Taxonomy Needs an Update for the AI Age – EdWeek](https://www.edweek.org/technology/opinion-blooms-taxonomy-needs-an-update-for-the-ai-age/2026/04)
